text: Welcome to Introduction to GIS for Environmental Science! Please review the syllabus linked in Materials and complete the Module 1 intro survey in the orientation workspace by Friday.
Reminder: Unit 2 attribute table quiz is this Monday. Review spatial joins and QGIS expression builder syntax. Office hours Thursday 5–6 PM HST via Zoom.

The georeferencing lab dataset (1978 NOAA Kaneohe Bay reef chart) is now uploaded to the course data folder. Download it before Tuesday. RMS error target is under 5 meters — use the harbor breakwater corners as stable GCPs.

No live session this Thursday — I am attending the Pacific GIS Consortium conference. Use the time to work on your Map Design project (cw_107). I have posted exemplary map layouts in Materials for reference.

Welcome to Spatial Analysis with QGIS and R! Make sure you have R 4.4+ and the sf terra spdep and tmap packages installed before our first live session. Prof. Okafor will lead the R portions starting in Unit 4.

Great work on the raster suitability models this week! Several of you found really thoughtful ways to weight the SST anomaly layer. I have posted three strong examples in Materials as study references.

Reminder: Marine spatial planning case study due Monday April 27. Make sure your map includes a proper CRS statement, north arrow, and scale bar. PDF exports only — QGIS project files are optional but encouraged.

Welcome to Remote Sensing Fundamentals! This module covers both the physics of remote sensing and hands-on image analysis. Come ready to work with real Sentinel-2 and Landsat imagery — we will start downloading data in Week 2.

Drone survey presentations start next Tuesday April 28. Sign up for your 5-minute presentation slot on the shared Google Sheet in Materials. Each presentation should include your proposed flight plan and accuracy expectations.

Module 3 final grades will be posted by June 20. Change detection papers are graded — overall strong work on the bleaching analysis. Feedback is in the gradebook comments. Please review before the Module 4 kickoff.

Welcome to the 2026 Pacific Environmental GIS Certificate cohort! All orientation tasks are due by January 12. Please complete QGIS and R installation first so we can troubleshoot any setup issues before the first live session on January 13.

Software setup tip: if QGIS fails to find the GDAL provider on macOS Sonoma, run the fix-GDAL.sh script posted in Materials under Software Setup. Several Mac users have hit this. Windows users should be fine with the standard installer.

Orientation complete — great work everyone! 24 of 26 students completed all tasks on time. The sample Kaneohe Bay dataset is a preview of the real data we will work with in the capstone. Module 1 starts Monday January 19.

Reminder: the December 2026 capstone deadline is firm — no extensions. Start identifying your capstone dataset early. If you are working with field-collected data from your own research be sure to review the CARE Principles reading before choosing how to represent community or indigenous knowledge in your maps.
Welcome to the 2025 Pacific Environmental GIS Certificate cohort orientation. All orientation tasks are due by January 14. QGIS 3.28 LTR is the required version for this cohort.

2025 orientation complete. All 22 students completed orientation tasks. The 2025 cohort ran through December — cohort graduates have been invited to the 2026 alumni network channel. Thank you all for a great year.

Unit 6 (spatial autocorrelation) lecture slides are now posted in Materials. The Moran's I worked example uses a reef bleaching dataset — should feel familiar if you've been following the Module 2 case study. Live session this Thursday 4 PM HST focuses on applying LISA cluster maps in R.

The Landsat change detection lab dataset is confirmed and uploaded (mat_015). Download the pre-processed image stack from Drive before Thursday — it is 1.4 GB so do not wait until the last minute. GEE script walkthrough will happen live in session 8.

Capstone check-in week begins June 16. Please post a one-paragraph status update in the Stream describing your dataset, your analysis approach, and any blockers. Dr. Tanaka will respond to each update by June 22. If you are using field-collected data from your own research remember to review the CARE Principles (mat_010) before including any community-derived datasets in your map.

Module 1 is now complete — great work on the georeferencing lab. Final grades will be posted by June 13. Before you start Module 2 make sure you have R 4.4+ installed. If you have never used R before spend some time with Geocomputation with R (mat_005) over the break — even chapters 1–3 will help enormously.
